content.text: Capability Heat Mapping Across Business Units

After identifying which value streams Claude can most impact, the next analytical step is narrower and more operationally concrete: mapping which specific Claude capabilities apply with what intensity to each business unit (BU). This capability heat map converts the broad stream-level impact assessment into a BU-level deployment guide that programme managers, platform engineers, and BU leads can act on directly.

A capability heat map is a two-dimensional grid with business units on one axis and Claude capability domains on the other. Each cell in the grid is scored to indicate the strength of fit between what that BU's core tasks look like and what that capability does well. The resulting map becomes the master reference for sequencing pilots, allocating integration effort, and communicating the programme's scope to BU stakeholders.

attrs.alt: Claude capability heat map showing High/Medium/Low intensity across Sales, Marketing, Engineering, Legal, HR, Finance, and Customer Support for seven core capability domains

content.text: The Seven Core Claude Capability Domains

Before building the heat map, the capability axis must be defined precisely. Vague labels like 'AI assistance' produce heatmaps that are not actionable. The seven domains that cover Claude's enterprise-relevant capabilities are:
Domain
What Claude Does
Representative Task
Text Generation
Drafts original prose from instructions, templates, or structured data
Proposal sections, job descriptions, email campaigns, policy drafts
Summarisation
Condenses long documents, transcripts, or data exports into structured key points
Meeting transcripts, lengthy contracts, research papers, customer call recordings
Data Extraction
Identifies and structures specific fields from unstructured text
Invoice line items, contract clauses, form data from PDFs, incident fields from logs
Q&A / Search
Answers questions from a provided context document or knowledge base
Internal policy lookup, product knowledge base queries, regulatory FAQs
Code Generation
Writes, explains, refactors, and tests code in major languages and frameworks
Feature implementation, test suite generation, SQL query writing, CI script authoring
Classification
Labels or categorises inputs according to a defined taxonomy
Ticket routing, sentiment scoring, contract type identification, risk tiering
Decision Support
Synthesises options, surfaces trade-offs, and drafts recommendations from evidence
Vendor selection analysis, escalation triage, scenario planning, hire/no-hire briefs
Resist the temptation to add more domains to appear comprehensive. Seven is enough to differentiate across BUs. More domains increase scoring complexity without improving deployment decisions.
The Three-Dimension Scoring Model
Each BU × capability domain cell is scored on three dimensions, then combined into a single composite score that drives the heat map colour:

attrs.alt: Scoring model diagram showing three dimensions: Task Fit (40%), Task Volume (35%), and BU Readiness (25%) combined into a composite heat map score

content.text: Dimension 1: Task Fit (40% weight)
Does the dominant task type in this BU, for this capability domain, match what Claude reliably performs well? Task fit is scored 1–5 using the same rubric from the value stream identification module:
5 — The capability is a core match: unambiguous language generation, summarisation, or extraction on well-bounded tasks
4 — Strong fit with minor prompt engineering to handle BU-specific terminology or output format
3 — Moderate fit; the BU uses the capability but at lower frequency or in more constrained scenarios
2 — Partial fit; the task partially matches but requires significant additional tooling (retrieval, structured data systems) to be reliable
1 — Weak fit; the task is numerical, rule-based, or requires real-time data Claude cannot access without integration
Dimension 2: Task Volume (35% weight)

How many instances of this capability type does the BU execute per year? Volume determines whether the fit translates into meaningful scale. A perfect-fit capability with ten annual instances does not justify integration investment.
5 — More than 10,000 instances per year (e.g. Customer Support with ticket volumes, Marketing with campaign content iterations)
4 — 1,000–10,000 instances per year (e.g. Sales proposal drafting, Legal contract review)
3 — 100–1,000 instances per year (typical for quarterly reporting, quarterly HR processes)
2 — 10–100 instances per year (annual strategy documents, annual vendor evaluations)
1 — Fewer than 10 instances per year (one-off regulatory submissions, M&A documents)
Dimension 3: BU Readiness (25% weight)

Is the business unit organisationally and technically ready to adopt this capability? A high-fit, high-volume cell that has an active BU champion, accessible data, and a defined integration path scores 5. A BU with resistant leadership or no data access scores 1, regardless of how attractive the technical fit is.
5 — Executive champion confirmed, use cases defined, data accessible, integration path identified
4 — Champion confirmed, use cases defined, data accessible (integration effort TBD)
3 — Expressed interest, no confirmed champion, data access unclear
2 — Leadership skeptical, active concerns about accuracy or job displacement raised
1 — Active resistance from BU leadership or critical data access barriers (legal hold, regulatory restriction)
Building the Heat Map: The Workshop Process

Heat maps built in isolation by a central team produce scores that BU leads dispute and ignore. Heat maps built collaboratively produce scores that BU leads own and act on. The recommended process is a structured two-hour workshop run once per major BU cluster:
Pre-work (one week before):

Send each BU lead a one-page briefing explaining the seven capability domains with one concrete example per domain drawn from their industry. Ask them to come with an estimate of the top three tasks in their BU where AI assistance might be relevant.
Opening (15 minutes):
Review the scoring model. Emphasise that scores are not performance reviews — a 'Low' score in Code Generation for the Finance BU is expected, not a criticism.
Domain scoring round (60 minutes):

Work through the heat map column by column. For each capability domain, ask: 'How many times per year does your team produce output in this category?' Then score volume, then discuss task fit based on examples. The facilitator records scores; BU leads vote or converge through brief discussion.
Readiness scoring round (30 minutes):

For any cell scoring 3+ on the combined Fit × Volume product, score BU Readiness. This layer is often the most sensitive — facilitators should frame resistance scores as 'what needs to change' rather than 'BU is a blocker'.
Priority identification (15 minutes):
Ask each BU to identify their top two cells as initial deployment candidates. This creates BU-owned priorities that the programme office can sequence without top-down mandates.
Reading the Heat Map: Four Deployment Signals
Once scored, the heat map produces four types of deployment signals that should drive programme decisions:
Signal 1: Bright Red Columns
What it means:

A capability domain that is red across most BUs indicates a horizontal capability — one where a single platform investment pays dividends everywhere. Text generation and Q&A / Search typically produce bright red columns in enterprise heat maps.
Programme action:

Build this capability into the central internal developer platform as a shared service. All BUs access it through a common API; each BU provides their own system prompt and context. Do not let each BU build separate integrations.
Signal 2: Bright Red Rows
What it means:

A business unit that scores red across many capability domains is a candidate for a comprehensive Claude programme — not just a point solution. Legal departments, Customer Support, and content-heavy Marketing functions often produce bright red rows.
Programme action:

Assign a dedicated deployment sprint to this BU. Scope a cross-capability integration (e.g. a Legal AI assistant that uses text generation, summarisation, extraction, and Q&A in a single workflow) rather than delivering capabilities one at a time.
Signal 3: Isolated Red Cells
What it means:

A cell that is red but surrounded by orange or grey indicates a targeted, high-value use case specific to that BU–capability combination. Finance Data Extraction (invoice parsing) and Engineering Code Generation are typical isolated red cells.
Programme action:

Develop these as standalone use cases with defined scope. They should not wait for a broader BU programme — they are fast, high-confidence deployments that demonstrate value quickly. Treat them as 'flagship pilots' that build credibility for the broader programme.
Signal 4: Grey Columns
What it means:

A capability domain that is consistently grey across all BUs is either a capability that requires infrastructure Claude cannot yet provide (real-time data access, numerical reasoning without calculation tools) or a domain that is already well-served by existing tooling.
Programme action:
Do not invest in integrating a grey-column capability in the current programme cycle. Flag it for re-evaluation when Claude gains additional capabilities or when existing tooling reaches end-of-life.
Calibrating Scores Against Observed Data
Workshop-generated scores are hypotheses, not measurements. Before locking the heat map, calibrate the top five cells against observed process data:

# Calibration check for a heat map cell
# Compare workshop estimate to observed task data

def calibrate_cell(bu_name, capability, workshop_volume_score, ticket_data):
    """
    Validates workshop volume score against actual task data.
    Returns calibrated score and confidence level.
    """
    observed_count = sum(
        1 for t in ticket_data
        if t["bu"] == bu_name
        and t["capability_tag"] == capability
    )

    # Map observed count to volume score scale
    if observed_count > 10000:
        observed_score = 5
    elif observed_count > 1000:
        observed_score = 4
    elif observed_count > 100:
        observed_score = 3
    elif observed_count > 10:
        observed_score = 2
    else:
        observed_score = 1

    delta = abs(workshop_volume_score - observed_score)
    confidence = "high" if delta <= 1 else "low"

    return {
        "bu": bu_name,
        "capability": capability,
        "workshop_score": workshop_volume_score,
        "observed_score": observed_score,
        "calibrated_score": observed_score,  # observed wins on disagreement
        "delta": delta,
        "confidence": confidence,
        "note": f"Observed {observed_count} instances; recalibrate if delta > 1"
    }

# Example output:
# {
#   "bu": "Customer Support",
#   "capability": "text_generation",
#   "workshop_score": 4,
#   "observed_score": 5,      # team underestimated ticket volume
#   "calibrated_score": 5,
#   "delta": 1,
#   "confidence": "high",
#   "note": "Observed 14,200 instances; recalibrate if delta > 1"
# }

When the delta between workshop estimate and observed data exceeds 1 point, hold a brief recalibration session with the BU lead before finalising the cell score. Disagreements of more than 1 point usually reveal that the BU lead was thinking of a different task boundary than the one the programme was scoring.
Heat Map Governance: Version Control and Refresh Cadence

A heat map that is produced once and never updated becomes misleading within six months. Three changes in that window can shift scores significantly: new Claude model releases improve capability-domain coverage; BU reorganisations change ownership and readiness scores; early deployment results update volume estimates as more tasks migrate to Claude.
Trigger
Scope of Update
Frequency
Major Claude model release (capability change)
Reassess all 'Low' task-fit cells that scored low due to capability limitations
On release
BU reorganisation or leadership change
Reassess BU Readiness scores for affected units
Within 30 days of change
First 6 months of deployment data available
Recalibrate Volume scores using observed task counts
6-month post-launch review
Annual programme strategy cycle
Full re-run of the workshop process; compare to prior year heat map
Annually

Maintain the heat map as a versioned artefact. Each version should record the assessment date, the participants in the scoring workshop, and the data sources used for calibration. This audit trail enables the programme to explain score changes to executives and prevents re-litigation of decisions already made.
Heat Map → Deployment Backlog

The heat map is an analytical artefact, not a deployment plan. Converting it into an actionable backlog requires one final step: for each cell scoring above the deployment threshold (composite score ≥ 3.5), create a backlog item with the following fields:
Use case name:
[BU] + [Capability Domain] + [Specific task] (e.g. 'Legal — Summarisation — Contract Summary for Deal Review')
Heat map cell score:
Composite score and breakdown by dimension
Deployment mode:
Augmentation (human reviews output) or automation (output used directly)
Integration requirements:
Data sources Claude needs access to; APIs or systems to connect
Owner:
Named individual in the BU responsible for requirements and UAT
Priority tier:
Tier 1 (score ≥ 4.0), Tier 2 (3.5–3.9), Tier 3 (below 3.5, deferred)

This backlog feeds the portfolio intake process. Every item has a source (the heat map cell), an owner (the BU lead), and a priority basis (the composite score). This makes programme sequencing defensible and prevents ad-hoc prioritisation by whoever shouts loudest.
Handling Difficult Scoring Conversations

The heat map workshop surfaces friction points that would otherwise remain hidden until deployment. Three specific conversations occur in nearly every enterprise heat map session and require deliberate facilitation:
'We don't want AI touching our outputs'

Legal, Finance, and regulated BUs frequently express concern that Claude-generated outputs will be used without review, creating liability or compliance exposure. This concern is not obstruction — it is accurate risk identification.

The right response is to clarify deployment mode in the scoring rubric: a cell scored 'High' in Legal text generation does not commit the BU to autonomous output. All Tier 1 deployments default to augmentation mode — Claude drafts, human reviews and approves, human takes accountability. Only Tier 3 (internal tooling like test generation) can move to automation without explicit legal sign-off.

When BUs hear 'High fit, augmentation mode' instead of 'High fit, automation', resistance typically drops by half. Document the deployment mode assumption in the heat map cell as a metadata field, not just in workshop notes.
'Our data is too sensitive for this'
Data sensitivity concerns are valid but often conflated with impossibility. The productive response is to clarify data residency and access controls before scoring BU Readiness:
Tier 1 data (public or internal-general):
No restrictions for Claude use. HR job descriptions, general marketing copy, non-confidential internal documentation.
Tier 2 data (confidential internal):
Claude API can process with appropriate API terms of service, contractual data processing agreements, and no-training-on-data clauses. Customer support tickets, internal policies, most contracts.
Tier 3 data (regulated or highly sensitive):

Requires legal review before any Claude use. PII, PHI, financial material non-public information, government classified. These restrict BU Readiness scores to 2 or below until an approved processing framework is in place.

A BU that claims all of its data is Tier 3 is either working in a genuinely highly regulated domain (uncommon) or has not completed a data classification exercise. In the latter case, the gap closure plan for that BU should include data classification as a prerequisite before the BU scores above 2 on readiness.
'How do we know Claude is accurate enough?'
Accuracy concerns reflect a gap in mental model: BU leads often compare Claude to a perfect expert rather than to the current error rate of the manual process. The productive framing is comparative:

The question is not 'Is Claude perfect?' The question is 'Is Claude's error rate lower than the current human error rate on this task, and is the failure mode acceptable?' If the answer is yes to both, deploy in augmentation mode.

Support this framing with a simple accuracy benchmark for any Tier 1 cell: run a 50-sample blind evaluation where the BU's domain expert rates Claude outputs and paired human-produced outputs without knowing which is which. Record the quality differential. This data resolves the accuracy concern with evidence rather than assertion.
Cross-BU Capability Clusters: Avoiding Redundant Integration Work

One of the most expensive failure modes in enterprise Claude programmes is when multiple BUs independently build integrations for the same Claude capability. The Sales team builds a proposal-drafting integration; the Marketing team builds a campaign-content integration; the HR team builds a job-description integration. All three use the same capability (text generation), built three times with three separate data pipelines, three separate prompt libraries, and three separate governance reviews.
The heat map prevents this when read across rows. When text generation scores High in three or more BUs:
Identify the common core: the shared capability (text generation), the shared output format (long-form prose), and the shared quality bar (professional-grade writing with BU-specific terminology)
Design a shared platform layer: a single Claude API wrapper with a pluggable BU context module — each BU provides its own system prompt, terminology glossary, and output template

Implement BU-specific customisation at the prompt level, not the integration level: the Sales BU's proposal prompts are different from HR's JD prompts, but both run through the same API endpoint, the same logging pipeline, and the same governance review process
Assign one platform engineering team to own the shared layer; assign BU-specific 'capability owners' to maintain the prompt libraries

This cross-BU cluster approach reduces total integration effort by 40–60% compared to independent BU implementations, and produces a shared governance footprint that is far easier to audit, secure, and maintain.
Heat Map for Subsidiary and Regional Entities

For enterprises with multiple subsidiaries, business lines, or regional entities, a single consolidated heat map obscures important variation. A region with a strong digital culture and modern data infrastructure may score 4–5 on BU Readiness while an acquired subsidiary still on legacy systems scores 1–2 on the same row.
The recommended approach is a two-level heat map structure:
Enterprise-level map (consolidated):
Uses the average or median score across all entities per cell. Drives enterprise-wide platform investment decisions. Identifies capability domains where a single shared service benefits all entities.
Entity-level map (per subsidiary or region):

Shows the individual score profile. Drives entity-specific deployment sequencing. Highlights where local infrastructure or readiness gaps require entity-specific preparation before they can benefit from enterprise-level platform investments.

Entities that score below 3 on BU Readiness in a cell that the enterprise map shows as red should not be forced into the enterprise deployment timeline. They should instead enter a readiness track that closes the infrastructure or change-management gap, with a target date to join the main deployment programme at the next cycle.

A cell that is red at the enterprise level but grey at a subsidiary level is not a deployment candidate for that subsidiary — it is a readiness gap closure target. Treat it as such in the gap closure plan.
Linking the Heat Map to OKRs and Budget Cycles
A heat map that exists as a standalone artefact rarely survives contact with annual budget planning. To protect its influence, the heat map must be connected to the budget process at two points:
Connecting Heat Map Scores to OKRs
For each Tier 1 cell (score ≥ 4.0), the programme should be able to map the deployment directly to at least one company-level OKR:
Heat Map Cell
Composite Score
Connected OKR
Expected Contribution
Customer Support — Summarisation
Reduce average handle time by 25%
Claude summaries eliminate re-reading; estimated 3 min/ticket saved on 14,000 tickets/month
Legal — Text Generation
Reduce time-to-contract by 30%
Claude first-draft reduces drafting time from 4 hours to 45 minutes for standard NDA templates
Engineering — Code Generation
Increase engineering velocity by 20%
Claude test generation and boilerplate reduction saves estimated 6 hrs/engineer/sprint
Sales — Q&A / Search
Increase proposal win rate by 10%
Faster, more accurate competitive-comparison sections in proposals reduce rep preparation time

This mapping is not a guarantee of OKR attainment — it is a hypothesis the deployment is designed to test. Document it as such: 'Deployment of Claude in Customer Support Summarisation is expected to contribute X% toward the AHT OKR, contingent on adoption by 80% of agents within 90 days of launch.'
Aligning the Heat Map to Annual Budget Cycles

Budget cycles happen on fixed schedules; Claude deployment opportunities do not. The heat map's role in the budget cycle is to provide pre-scored, pre-sequenced deployment options that can be packaged into funding requests:
Q1 (planning cycle):

Present the heat map to BU CFOs and the central technology budget owner. Request Tier 1 cell deployments as line items in BU budgets, not as a central IT request. BUs that own the budget own the adoption outcome.
Q2 (mid-year review):

Report actual progress against the Tier 1 cells funded in Q1. Use this data to refine heat map scores (particularly BU Readiness, which may have improved or declined) and to request budget for Tier 2 cells in the next cycle.
Q3 (pre-planning preparation):

Refresh the heat map with six-month observed data and updated BU Readiness scores. Prepare the Tier 1 cell shortlist for the next annual budget cycle with updated OKR linkage and investment estimates.
Q4 (budget submission):
Submit the refreshed Tier 1 cell deployments as funded programme line items for the following year, with the heat map as supporting evidence for prioritisation and ROI projection.
Summary
Capability heat mapping

translates value stream analysis into BU-level deployment intelligence. The heat map answers the question every BU leader will ask: 'What specifically does Claude do for my team, and how much does it matter?'
Score each BU × capability domain cell on task fit, task volume, and BU readiness — weight at 40/35/25
Build the map collaboratively in workshops; BU-owned scores drive adoption, analyst-owned scores drive resistance
Interpret red columns as platform investments, red rows as BU programmes, isolated red cells as flagship pilots
Calibrate workshop scores against observed process data before locking the map
Maintain the map as a versioned, governed artefact with a defined refresh cadence
Convert cells above the deployment threshold (score ≥ 3.5) directly into the portfolio backlog

Enterprises that complete this mapping exercise consistently identify three to five high-confidence deployment opportunities per BU within the first six months — opportunities that would have taken a year or more to surface through bottom-up experimentation. The heat map accelerates the convergence from 'we are exploring AI' to 'we are deploying AI at scale.'
